shot.subject: grass
shot.notes: from the meadow floor, straight up: blades framing a burning blue dome

segments.text: Every citizen of this garden lives under the same roof. The ant glimpses it between towers. The fish reads it through a trembling ceiling. The birds fly in its lowest room. And that roof is not empty space — it is a substance, a thin shell of gas wrapped around the world, doing a dozen invisible jobs at once.

shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: the full dome, horizon to horizon, over the small complete world

segments.text: We call it air, and treat it as nothing. It is very far from nothing.

shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: a slow rotation across the whole heaven

shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: clean midday blue, the dome at its emptiest and deepest

segments.text: Fill your lungs and you have taken in a very specific recipe. Roughly four molecules in five are nitrogen — an unreactive gas that mostly just fills the space. About one in five is oxygen, the reactive one every animal here burns to stay alive. The rest is a trace: a whisper of argon, a little carbon dioxide, and a variable amount of water vapor. That thin mixture is what fire needs to burn, what the flower breathes in, and what the ant, the bird, and the Visitor cannot survive three minutes without.

shot.subject: flower
shot.notes: a bloom exchanging gases with the still air around it

segments.text: And it circulates. Plants pull in carbon dioxide and release oxygen in the light; animals do the reverse. Every breath in the garden is a trade with the same shared atmosphere — the flower exhaling what the beetle inhales, over and over, all day.

shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: rising from the grass tops up through the lowest, weathered layer of air

segments.text: The roof has floors. The one we live on is the lowest and densest — the troposphere, a shallow layer hugging the ground where nearly all the water vapor rides. Every cloud, every rainfall, every gust the garden ever feels happens in this bottom room. Weather is a ground-floor event.

shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: high thin haze far above the weather, catching the last clean light

segments.text: Above it lies a calmer layer, the stratosphere, and folded into that upper air is a faint band of a special form of oxygen — the ozone layer. It is a shield. It soaks up most of the sun's harshest ultraviolet light before that radiation can reach the meadow and burn the living things below. The garden basks in sunlight because, high overhead, an invisible filter has already strained out the part that would harm it.

shot.subject: sun
shot.notes: light flooding across the dome, the whole sky lit an even blue

segments.text: Now the question no resident ever asks: why is it blue at all? Sunlight looks white but carries every color mixed together. As it pours through the air, it collides with countless molecules — and the smaller, bluer waves of light get knocked sideways, scattered across the whole dome, far more than the reds. So blue light arrives at your eye from every direction at once. The sky is not painted. It is white sunlight, with the blue shaken loose and spread everywhere.

shot.subject: sun
shot.notes: the sun sinking toward the rim, its light reddening as it goes

segments.text: Watch the same trick reverse at dusk. When the sun drops to the rim, its light must slice through a far longer wedge of air to reach you. The blue is scattered away entirely before it arrives, leaving only the deep reds and golds to come through straight. Every sunset the garden has ever shown is the same physics as the noon blue, read from the far end. The lamp does not change color. The air does the painting.

shot.subject: field
shot.notes: soft even warmth lying over the whole meadow, no shadow harsh

segments.text: The air does a subtler job than any of these — it holds the heat. Certain gases in it, carbon dioxide and water vapor chief among them, let the sun's light in but slow the escape of the warmth the ground gives back. That gentle trapping — the greenhouse effect — keeps the garden's nights from plunging to killing cold. Without the blanket of air, this world would swing from scorching day to frozen dark. The atmosphere is the reason the meadow has a livable temperature at all.

shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: high cloud drifting, the air visibly moving across the dome

segments.text: And it is never still. The sun heats the ground unevenly — bright hillside warmer than shaded hollow — and warm air rises while cooler air slides in beneath to take its place. That flow is wind: air moving from where pressure is high toward where it is low, chasing the sun's uneven touch. Every breeze that stirs the grass is the atmosphere balancing its own books.

shot.subject: birds
shot.notes: the flock riding a current of moving air across the dome

segments.text: For the living, the air is also a highway and a mail service. It carries the birds and the buzzing insects. It ferries pollen from flower to flower and lifts seeds to new ground. It moves the whole water cycle — evaporation up, rain and snow down. And it is a shield against more than sunlight: the same blanket burns up most of the small debris that would otherwise strike the ground from above.

shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: gliding beneath a darkening cloud base as the last blue closes

segments.text: And when this restless air stacks up tall and cold and charged, it finds its loudest voice.

shot.subject: lightning
shot.notes: a single stroke splits a bruised sky over the far field

segments.text: That voice — thunder, and the storm that carries it — earns an hour of its own another day. For now, the roof holds, and the garden breathes.

shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: rising into deepening blue as the first stars pierce through

segments.text: But turn the lamp off, and the roof does something no daytime dome can. The blue was only ever the air, lit from in front. Switch off the sun, and you can finally see straight through the air — out, and out, to the lights that were always burning behind it.

shot.subject: stars
shot.notes: the dome crowded with stars over the black field
